comments.text: Erdős and Graham say they don't know any upper bound. I believe I can give an upper bound of $x^{ O(1/\sqrt{\log \log x})}$. For a random integer in an interval $I$ of length $L$, it is straightforward to compute the first three moments of the number of prime factors $<L^{1/3}$: The mean is $\log \log L^{1/3}+ o(1)$, the variance is $(1+o(1) \log \log L^{1/3}$, and the skewness is $o( (\log \log L^{1/3})^{3/2})$. These moments are incompatible with a distribution supported on $ ( \log \log L^{1/3} - \delta \sqrt{ \log \log L^{1/3}},\infty)$ for all $\delta$ sufficiently small, and thus there must be some number in $I$ whose number of prime factor $<L^{1/3}$ is at most $\log \log L^{1/3} - \delta \sqrt{ \log \log L^{1/3}}$. Now the remaining prime factors must all be at least $L^{1/3}$, so the number of them is $\log (2x)/\log (L^{1/3})=3 \log(2x)/\log L$ so that if we choose $L$ so that $3\log(2x)/\log L> \delta \sqrt{\log \log L^{1/3}}$ then there must be numbers in $I$ with $<\log \log L^{1/3}<\log \log x$ prime factors. It suffices to choose $L$ with $3\log(2x)/\log L> \delta \sqrt{\log \log x^{1/3}}$ which gives $L= x^{ O(1/\sqrt{\log \log x})}$. The reverse problem where one looks for $I$ with $\omega(n)<\log \log n$ for all $n\in I$ also seems interesting. The upper bound is much stronger: As soon as $I$ is longer than the product of the first $\log \log 2x$ primes, which. has size $\log x^{ (1+o(1)) \log \log \log x}$, some number in $I$ divisible by the product of the first $\log \log 2x$ primes and hence has at least $\log \log 2x$ prime factors. A lower bound can be obtained by sieving out small prime factors but I'm not sure exactly what you get.
